questions.q: In the Puranas, Amaravati is the heavenly capital of which deva?
questions.explanation: Amaravati is described in the Puranas as the resplendent capital of Indra, king of the devas.

questions.q: Amaravati is the capital of which heavenly realm ruled by Indra?
questions.explanation: Amaravati is the capital of Svarga, the heaven over which Indra presides as king of the devas.

questions.q: On which celestial white elephant does Indra ride in Amaravati?
questions.explanation: Indra is depicted presiding mounted on the white elephant Airavata, his celestial vahana.

questions.q: What is the name of the famed pleasure garden within Indra's heaven Amaravati?
questions.explanation: Nandana is the celebrated celestial pleasure garden of Indra's heaven, described in the Puranas.

questions.q: Which wish-granting tree is said to stand in Indra's heaven of Amaravati?
questions.explanation: The Parijata, a wish-granting celestial tree, is described as standing in Indra's heaven.

questions.q: Indra, the ruler of Amaravati, is known in the Vedas as the king of which class of celestial beings?
questions.explanation: Indra is hailed throughout the Vedas as the king of the devas, the celestial gods.

questions.q: According to the Puranas, who dwells in Amaravati and enjoy the fruits of their merit?
questions.explanation: Amaravati is the paradise where virtuous souls enjoy the fruits of their accumulated merit.

questions.q: The celestial dancers who entertain in Indra's court at Amaravati are known by which name?
questions.explanation: The apsaras are the celestial dancers of Indra's court, famed for their beauty and artistry.

questions.q: In the Puranas, what kind of place is Amaravati primarily described as?
questions.explanation: Amaravati is described as the heavenly city, the resplendent capital of Indra's Svarga.

questions.q: According to the Puranas, the souls dwelling in Amaravati eventually do what?
options: Return to the cycle of rebirth
questions.explanation: Souls enjoy heaven's pleasures until their merit is spent, then return to the cycle of rebirth.

questions.q: The wish-granting Parijata tree of Indra's heaven is said to have been obtained during which great event in the Puranas?
options: The Samudra Manthana (churning of the ocean)
questions.explanation: The Parijata is among the treasures said to have emerged from the Samudra Manthana, the churning of the ocean.

questions.q: In the Bhagavata Purana, Krishna famously carried off the Parijata tree from Indra's heaven for which of his queens?
questions.explanation: The Bhagavata Purana relates that Krishna brought the Parijata from Indra's heaven for his queen Satyabhama.

questions.q: Who is described in the Puranas as the consort of Indra, queen of Amaravati?
questions.explanation: Shachi, also called Indrani, is the consort of Indra and queen of his heavenly city.

questions.q: Airavata, the white elephant of Indra at Amaravati, is also said in the Puranas to have arisen from which event?
questions.explanation: Airavata is counted among the wondrous beings that emerged from the Samudra Manthana.

questions.q: Which thunderbolt weapon does Indra, lord of Amaravati, wield according to the Vedas and Puranas?
questions.explanation: Indra wields the Vajra, the thunderbolt, his signature weapon throughout the Vedas and Puranas.

questions.q: In the Vedas, Indra of the celestial heaven is most celebrated for slaying which serpent-demon to release the waters?
questions.explanation: The Rigveda's central myth praises Indra for slaying Vritra with his Vajra to release the pent-up waters.

questions.q: The name 'Amaravati' is associated with the dwelling of the 'amaras.' What does 'amara' mean?
questions.explanation: 'Amara' means 'deathless' or 'immortal,' a term for the devas, hence Amaravati, city of the immortals.

questions.q: In the Ramayana, when Ravana wages war upon the heavens, against which king's celestial city and forces does he campaign?
questions.explanation: The Ramayana recounts Ravana's assault upon Indra's heaven, where his son even captures Indra, earning the name Indrajit.

questions.q: Ravana's son in the Ramayana earned the title 'Indrajit' for what feat connected to Indra's heaven?
questions.explanation: Meghanada earned the name Indrajit, 'conqueror of Indra,' after defeating and binding Indra in battle.

questions.q: In Hindu cosmology, Amaravati as Indra's heaven is reached by souls through what means?
questions.explanation: The Puranas teach that virtuous deeds yield punya (merit), by which souls attain Indra's heaven.

questions.q: Which sage's curse, recounted in the Puranas, is often linked to Indra's troubles, reflecting that even the lord of Amaravati is bound by karma?
questions.explanation: The Puranas relate that the sage Gautama cursed Indra, showing that even heaven's king is subject to consequence.

questions.q: In the directional scheme of the Puranas, Indra, lord of Amaravati, is the guardian (Lokapala) of which cardinal direction?
questions.explanation: Indra is the Lokapala of the East among the guardians of the directions in Puranic cosmology.

questions.q: In Indra's assembly hall at Amaravati, described in the Mahabharata, which celestial musicians sing alongside the apsaras?
questions.explanation: The Mahabharata describes Gandharvas, the celestial musicians, performing with the apsaras in Indra's court.

questions.q: The Mahabharata describes a sage visiting Indra's heaven and its assembly hall; which Pandava ascended to spend time with his father Indra there?
questions.explanation: Arjuna, son of Indra, ascended to his father's heaven in the Mahabharata to receive celestial weapons.

questions.q: According to the Puranas, which divine architect is credited with fashioning the splendid structures of the devas, including Indra's heavenly city?
questions.explanation: Vishvakarma, the architect of the devas, is credited in the Puranas with building the celestial city and halls.

questions.q: Indra's celestial chariot, used about his heavenly realm, is driven by which charioteer in the Puranas?
questions.explanation: Matali is named in the epics and Puranas as the faithful charioteer of Indra.

questions.q: The river said to flow through or near Indra's heaven, the celestial Ganga, is known in the Puranas by which name in its heavenly course?
questions.explanation: In its heavenly course the Ganga is called Mandakini or Akasha Ganga, the celestial river of the gods.

questions.q: According to the Puranas, the Vajra wielded by Indra, lord of Amaravati, was forged from the bones of which self-sacrificing sage?
questions.explanation: The Puranas relate that Indra's thunderbolt, the Vajra, was fashioned from the bones of the sage Dadhichi, who gave up his body for the good of the devas.

questions.q: In the Puranas, the position of Indra, king of Amaravati, is held to be not eternal but occupied across ages by different beings. What is this office titled?
questions.explanation: The Puranas describe Indra-pada, the post of Indra, as a station held by different worthy beings across the cosmic ages.

questions.q: The amrita-bearing drink of immortality enjoyed by the devas of Amaravati was obtained, per the Puranas, through which collaborative effort?
options: Devas and asuras churning the ocean
questions.explanation: The amrita was won when devas and asuras together churned the cosmic ocean in the Samudra Manthana.
